section.title: Duties and Portions of the Priests
section.sectionRationale: Closing the long unit that began with Korah's rebellion and Aaron's budded staff, this chapter settles the practical question those crises raised, who bears the risk and the responsibility of the sanctuary, and answers it with generous provision: the priests and Levites will have no land inheritance among the tribes because the LORD Himself, and the tithes given to Him, will be their portion.
section.thesis: Aaron and his sons bear the weighty responsibility for any offense involving the sanctuary, while the Levites assist them, and in exchange for this life devoted entirely to God's service, both priests and Levites receive their portion not in land but directly from God's own provision, the sacred offerings and the tithe, showing that those set apart to serve God most closely find their true inheritance in Him rather than in property.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Aaron and his sons bearing the responsibility for the sanctuary and priesthood, with the Levites given to assist and protect them; the priests' portions from the holy and sacred offerings of Israel; the LORD Himself declared to be Aaron's portion and inheritance, with the Levites given the tithe instead of land; and the Levites required to give a tithe of their tithe to the priests.

units.label: Aaron and the Levites bear the responsibility

verses.text: So the LORD said to Aaron, “You and your sons and your father's house must bear the iniquity involving the sanctuary. And you and your sons alone must bear the iniquity involving your priesthood.
But bring with you also your brothers from the tribe of Levi, the tribe of your father, that they may join you and assist you and your sons before the Tent of the Testimony.
And they shall attend to your duties and to all the duties of the Tent; but they must not come near to the furnishings of the sanctuary or the altar, or both they and you will die.
They are to join you and attend to the duties of the Tent of Meeting, doing all the work at the Tent; but no outsider may come near you.
And you shall attend to the duties of the sanctuary and of the altar, so that wrath may not fall on the Israelites again.
Behold, I Myself have selected your fellow Levites from the Israelites as a gift to you, dedicated to the LORD to perform the service for the Tent of Meeting.

But only you and your sons shall attend to your priesthood for everything concerning the altar and what is inside the veil, and you are to perform that service. I am giving you the work of the priesthood as a gift, but any outsider who comes near the sanctuary must be put to death.”

units.label: The priests' portions from the offerings

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Aaron, “Behold, I have put you in charge of My offerings. As for all the sacred offerings of the Israelites, I have given them to you and your sons as a portion and a permanent statute.

A portion of the most holy offerings reserved from the fire will be yours. From all the offerings they render to Me as most holy offerings, whether grain offerings or sin offerings or guilt offerings, that part belongs to you and your sons.
You are to eat it as a most holy offering, and every male may eat it. You shall regard it as holy.

And this is yours as well: the offering of their gifts, along with all the wave offerings of the Israelites. I have given this to you and your sons and daughters as a permanent statute. Every ceremonially clean person in your household may eat it.
I give you all the freshest olive oil and all the finest new wine and grain that the Israelites give to the LORD as their firstfruits.
The firstfruits of everything in their land that they bring to the LORD will belong to you. Every ceremonially clean person in your household may eat them.
Every devoted thing in Israel belongs to you.
The firstborn of every womb, whether man or beast, that is offered to the LORD belongs to you. But you must surely redeem every firstborn son and every firstborn male of unclean animals.
You are to pay the redemption price for a month-old male according to your valuation: five shekels of silver, according to the sanctuary shekel, which is twenty gerahs.

But you must not redeem the firstborn of an ox, a sheep, or a goat; they are holy. You are to sprinkle their blood on the altar and burn their fat as an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
And their meat belongs to you, just as the breast and right thigh of the wave offering belong to you.

All the holy offerings that the Israelites present to the LORD I give to you and to your sons and daughters as a permanent statute. It is a permanent covenant of salt before the LORD for you and your offspring.”

units.label: The LORD is Aaron's inheritance
units.note: the Levites given the tithe instead of land

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Aaron, “You will have no {{inheritance|inheritance}} in their land, nor will you have any portion among them. I am your portion and your inheritance among the Israelites.
Behold, I have given to the Levites all the tithes in Israel as an inheritance in return for the work they do, the service of the Tent of Meeting.
No longer may the Israelites come near to the Tent of Meeting, or they will incur guilt and die.

The Levites are to perform the work of the Tent of Meeting, and they must bear their iniquity. This is a permanent statute for the generations to come. The Levites will not receive an inheritance among the Israelites.

For I have given to the Levites as their inheritance the tithe that the Israelites present to the LORD as a contribution. That is why I told them that they would not receive an inheritance among the Israelites.”

units.label: The Levites' tithe of the tithe

verses.text: And the LORD instructed Moses,

“Speak to the Levites and tell them: ‘When you receive from the Israelites the tithe that I have given you as your inheritance, you must present part of it as an offering to the LORD, a tithe of the tithe.
Your offering will be reckoned to you as grain from the threshing floor or juice from the winepress.
So you are to present an offering to the LORD from all the tithes you receive from the Israelites, and from these you are to give the LORD's offering to Aaron the priest.
You must present the offering due the LORD from all the best of every gift, the holiest part of it.’
Therefore say to the Levites, ‘When you have presented the best part, it will be reckoned to you as the produce of the threshing floor or winepress.
And you and your households may eat the rest of it anywhere; it is the compensation for your work at the Tent of Meeting.
Once you have presented the best part of it, you will not incur guilt because of it. But you must not defile the sacred offerings of the Israelites, or else you will die.’”

inheritance.gloss: Inheritance or portion, the same word normally used for tribal land allotments in Canaan. Declaring that the LORD Himself is Aaron's nachalah deliberately substitutes God for territory, reframing what could look like exclusion from land as instead the greatest possible inheritance.

basic.context: As the long section of rebellion narratives closes, God settles the practical arrangements for priestly and Levitical support: Aaron and his sons bear full responsibility for any offense involving the sanctuary, the Levites assist and protect them from unauthorized approach, and in place of a land inheritance among the tribes, the priests receive a portion of Israel's sacred offerings while the Levites receive the tithe, out of which they in turn give a tenth to the priests.
basic.hermeneutics: This is practical, historical legislation establishing the material support system for Israel's priesthood and Levites. Its key theological claim, that the LORD Himself is Aaron's portion and inheritance rather than land, verse 20, reframes what might look like a merely administrative arrangement as a statement about where true security and identity are ultimately found.
basic.originalLanguages: The declaration that the LORD is Aaron's nachalah, portion and inheritance, uses the very term normally used for tribal land allotments in Canaan, deliberately substituting God Himself for territory, so that Aaron's priestly line possesses something greater than any plot of ground, the LORD's own presence and provision as their lasting share.
basic.typology: The priesthood receiving no land inheritance, but the LORD Himself as their portion, anticipates believers whose true inheritance is not an earthly possession but God Himself, kept for them in heaven, and ultimately anticipates Christ, our great High Priest, whose reward is not territorial but relational, seated at the Father's right hand.
basic.christ: Where Aaron and his sons bore the weighty risk of guilt involving the sanctuary and depended entirely on God's provision rather than land, Christ, our true and greater High Priest, bore the full weight of guilt for sin once for all, Hebrews 9:26, and now, having no need of any earthly inheritance, has been given the name above every name and reigns as our eternal mediator.
basic.god: God provides generously for those He calls to serve Him closely, ensuring the priests and Levites are cared for through the very offerings brought to Him rather than left to fend for themselves. He also holds them to serious responsibility, since bearing the guilt of the sanctuary is no light matter, showing that closeness to God's service carries both privilege and weight.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 10:9 and 18:1-2 echo this chapter's declaration that the LORD, not land, is the Levites' inheritance. Hebrews 9:26 describes Christ's once-for-all bearing of sin, surpassing the priests' ongoing responsibility here. 1 Peter 1:4 describes believers' inheritance kept in heaven, incorruptible and unfading.
basic.oneStory: God provides generously for those set apart to serve Him, giving them Himself as their true portion rather than land, and requiring careful responsibility in exchange for the privilege of nearness to His holy things. That pattern of costly service and God-given inheritance culminates in Christ, our great High Priest, who bore the full weight of sin and now shares His inheritance with all who are united to Him by faith.

context.literary: This chapter closes the long unit running from Korah's rebellion through Aaron's budded staff, resolving the practical question those crises raised about who legitimately bears the burden and receives the support of priestly and Levitical service.
context.historical: By receiving no tribal land allotment, the priests and Levites were structurally dependent on the rest of Israel's faithfulness in bringing tithes and offerings, a system that both honored their unique calling and tied their material welfare directly to the nation's ongoing obedience.
context.canonical: The principle that the LORD Himself, not land, is the true inheritance of those who serve Him closely echoes forward through the Psalms, notably Psalm 16:5, and into the New Testament's description of every believer's imperishable inheritance kept in heaven, 1 Peter 1:4.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish clearly the respective duties and material support of the priests and Levites, framing their lack of land inheritance not as deprivation but as a unique, God-given portion superior to any territorial allotment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter describes the specific historical support system for Israel's priests and Levites; it does not prescribe an identical tithe-based support structure for church leadership today, though the underlying principle, those who serve at the altar should share in its offerings, is affirmed and applied in 1 Corinthians 9:13-14. Its abiding principle is that God provides for those who serve Him and that He Himself, not material possession, is the deepest reward of such service.

debates.question: Why does the chapter emphasize so strongly that the LORD, not land, is Aaron's inheritance, verse 20, when land inheritance was so central to the rest of Israel's tribal identity?

views.label: A deliberate theological statement elevating priestly calling above material wealth
views.case: By explicitly denying land to the priestly line while every other tribe received territory, the text elevates nearness to God Himself as a greater, though less tangible, form of inheritance than any plot of Canaan.

views.label: A practical necessity given the priests' unique national, rather than territorial, role
views.case: Since priests served the whole nation from various assigned cities rather than one tribal territory, Numbers 35, this arrangement also reflects the practical logistics of a priesthood meant to serve all Israel rather than being tied to one region.

debates.bearing: Both explanations converge on the same enduring truth: the text presents having God Himself as one's portion as a greater good than territorial possession, a truth the New Testament extends to every believer's inheritance in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The declaration that the LORD is Aaron's nachalah, portion and inheritance, deliberately uses the very term applied elsewhere to the tribal land allotments in Canaan, substituting God Himself for territory and reframing what could look like exclusion from a real material blessing as instead the greatest possible inheritance.

typology.type: The priesthood receiving the LORD Himself, not land, as their inheritance
typology.fulfillment: Believers' inheritance kept in heaven, incorruptible and unfading, 1 Peter 1:4, and ultimately God Himself as the believer's portion, Psalm 16:5.

typology.type: Aaron and his sons bearing the weighty responsibility of the sanctuary's guilt
typology.fulfillment: Christ, our great High Priest, who bore the full weight of sin once for all, Hebrews 9:26.

study.christ: Where Aaron and his sons bore the weighty risk of guilt involving the sanctuary and depended entirely on God's provision rather than land, Christ, our true and greater High Priest, bore the full weight of guilt for sin once for all, Hebrews 9:26, and now, having no need of any earthly inheritance, has been given the name above every name and reigns as our eternal mediator.
study.god: God provides generously for those He calls to serve Him closely, ensuring the priests and Levites are cared for through the very offerings brought to Him rather than left to fend for themselves. He also holds them to serious responsibility, since bearing the guilt of the sanctuary is no light matter, showing that closeness to God's service carries both privilege and weight.

crossReferences.note: Echoes this chapter's declaration that the LORD, not land, is the tribe of Levi's inheritance.
Describes Christ's once-for-all bearing of sin, surpassing the priests' ongoing responsibility described in this chapter.
Describes believers' inheritance kept in heaven, incorruptible and unfading, echoing this chapter's reframing of true inheritance.
Applies the principle that those who serve at the altar share in its offerings to the support of gospel ministers today.

study.oneStory: God provides generously for those set apart to serve Him, giving them Himself as their true portion rather than land, and requiring careful responsibility in exchange for the privilege of nearness to His holy things. That pattern of costly service and God-given inheritance culminates in Christ, our great High Priest, who bore the full weight of sin and now shares His inheritance with all who are united to Him by faith.

quiz.q: What responsibility did Aaron and his sons bear according to the opening of this chapter?

options.text: The iniquity involving the sanctuary and their priesthood, so that no outsider could approach without guilt.
options.note: Verses 1 and 7 state this responsibility exactly.

options.text: The responsibility of leading Israel's army in battle.
options.note: This is not the responsibility described in this chapter.

options.text: The responsibility of collecting the census figures each year.
options.note: This is not described anywhere in this chapter.

options.text: No specific responsibility is described.
options.note: Verses 1 and 7 describe this responsibility directly.

quiz.q: What role did the Levites play according to this chapter?

options.text: To join Aaron and his sons, assisting with the duties of the Tent of Meeting, though not permitted to approach the furnishings of the sanctuary or the altar themselves.
options.note: Verses 2 to 4 describe this exact role and its limits.

options.text: To serve as the only ones permitted to enter the Most Holy Place.
options.note: This contradicts the text; that access belonged only to the high priest, not the Levites generally.

options.text: To replace the priesthood entirely going forward.
options.note: This contradicts the ongoing distinction between priests and Levites maintained throughout the chapter.

options.text: No role for the Levites is described.
options.note: Verses 2 to 4 describe their role in detail.

quiz.q: What did the priests receive instead of a land inheritance among the Israelites?

options.text: A portion of the sacred offerings, including the most holy offerings and the firstfruits.
options.note: Verses 8 to 19 describe this provision in detail.

options.text: A fixed annual salary paid directly from a royal treasury.
options.note: This is not the arrangement described in verses 8 to 19.

options.text: Personal ownership of the tabernacle and its furnishings.
options.note: This is not stated anywhere in the text.

options.text: No alternative provision is described.
options.note: Verses 8 to 19 describe the provision in full.

quiz.q: What did God declare Himself to be for Aaron, according to verse 20?

options.text: His portion and his inheritance among the Israelites, since he would have no land inheritance.
options.note: Verse 20 states this declaration exactly.

options.text: His only source of physical protection in battle.
options.note: This is not the content of verse 20.

options.text: His personal servant for daily tasks.
options.note: This is not stated anywhere in the text.

options.text: No such declaration is made in this chapter.
options.note: Verse 20 makes this declaration directly.

quiz.q: What were the Levites required to do with the tithes they received from the Israelites?

options.text: Present a tithe of the tithe as an offering to the LORD, giving this portion to Aaron the priest.
options.note: Verses 26 to 28 describe this requirement exactly.

options.text: Keep the entire tithe for themselves with no further obligation.
options.note: This contradicts the explicit instruction to tithe from their tithe in verses 26 to 28.

options.text: Redistribute the entire tithe equally among all twelve tribes.
options.note: This contradicts the specific instruction given to the Levites regarding Aaron's portion.

options.text: No further instruction about the tithe is given.
options.note: Verses 26 to 28 give further instruction directly.

questions: Aaron and his sons bore serious, weighty responsibility for the sanctuary, a privilege that came with real risk. Where has God given you a responsibility that carries more weight than you initially realized, and how are you stewarding it?

The Levites assisted the priests but were not permitted to do everything the priests did, each serving within defined boundaries. How do you handle boundaries on your own role, especially when you might want to do more than you have been given to do?

God declared Himself to be Aaron's portion and inheritance rather than any plot of land. What would it look like for you to genuinely believe that having God Himself is a greater inheritance than any material security you are tempted to chase instead?

The priests depended entirely on the rest of Israel's faithfulness in bringing tithes and offerings for their material support. Who in your life depends on your faithfulness in giving or generosity, and are you providing for them as you should?

Even the Levites, who received the tithe, were required to tithe from what they received, showing that generosity flows all the way through, not stopping once you have received something yourself. Where might God be calling you to keep passing on generosity rather than treating what you receive as a final stopping point?

translationNotes.note: Bear the iniquity involving the sanctuary indicates Aaron and his family assume legal and spiritual responsibility for any failure in properly handling holy things, a serious weight attached to their unique access.
A permanent covenant of salt uses an ancient idiom for an unbreakable, enduring agreement, salt being a stable, incorruptible substance associated elsewhere with covenant meals and preservation.

All the tithes in Israel given to the Levites as their inheritance in place of land directly explains why later biblical books frequently mention the Levites alongside the poor, the widow, and the orphan as those requiring the community's active, ongoing generosity.
